title: Supporting Your Team Through Baltic Winters
subtitle: When sunrise is at 8:45 and sunset at 15:50, your team needs more than a coffee break.

toc.label: The Winter Challenge Nobody Talks About

tools: T220-01 · SAD Awareness and Support Guide

metrics.label: of Baltic population affected by SAD

sections.heading: The Winter Challenge Nobody Talks About
sections.body:
In December in Vilnius, the sun rises at 8:45 and sets at 15:50. That is 7 hours and 5 minutes of daylight — and much of it is overcast. A barista who starts at 6:30am arrives in darkness. If their shift ends at 15:00, they leave in near-darkness. They may not see sunlight for days.

January is barely better. The cold adds another layer: average temperatures of -5°C, with regular dips to -15°C or -20°C. The commute is harsh. The streets are icy. The motivation to leave a warm bed for a 6:30am start is genuinely difficult.

This is not about being tough or soft. Seasonal affective disorder (SAD) affects 10–20% of people in northern latitudes. A much larger percentage experiences sub-clinical winter blues — lower energy, reduced motivation, increased irritability, and a craving for sleep and carbohydrates.

In a cafe team of 6, statistically 1–2 people are meaningfully affected. The symptoms look like performance problems — lateness, mistakes, withdrawal — but the cause is biochemical, not attitudinal.

The cafe owner who recognises this and responds with support rather than discipline retains staff through winter. The one who treats winter fatigue as laziness loses people — often their best people, who have options and choose to leave rather than endure.

Winter wellbeing is not a luxury. In the Baltics, it is an operational necessity.

sections.heading: Recognising Seasonal Affective Disorder
sections.body:
SAD is a clinical condition triggered by reduced sunlight exposure. It affects serotonin production and circadian rhythm. It is not sadness — it is a physiological response to darkness.

**Symptoms to watch for in your team (October–March):**

- **Persistent fatigue.** Not just tired after a long shift — tired before the shift starts. Struggling to wake up, low energy throughout the day, needing more sleep than usual.
- **Mood changes.** Withdrawal from team interactions, reduced enthusiasm, increased irritability, emotional sensitivity disproportionate to the situation.
- **Concentration difficulty.** More mistakes than usual, slower processing, forgetting steps in familiar routines.
- **Appetite changes.** Craving carbohydrates and sugar. Weight gain. Skipping meals or overeating.
- **Absence increase.** More sick days, especially Mondays. Arriving late more frequently.

**What SAD is NOT:**
- It is not laziness (the person wants to be energetic but cannot)
- It is not a bad attitude (their personality has not changed — their brain chemistry has)
- It is not permanent (symptoms lift naturally as daylight increases in March–April)
- It is not rare (in Lithuania's latitude, it is common enough that you should expect it)

**How to respond when you notice symptoms:**

Do not diagnose. Do not say "I think you have SAD." Instead: "I've noticed you seem lower energy recently. Is everything okay? Is there anything I can do to help?" Open the door. Let them walk through it.

If they acknowledge struggling with the dark months, you can suggest practical adjustments (next section) and share information about SAD resources. Vilnius has mental health services available through both the public health system and private practitioners.

sections.heading: Workplace Adjustments
sections.body:
You cannot change the weather. You can change the workplace environment to mitigate winter's impact.

**Lighting:**
This is the single most impactful adjustment. SAD is driven by light deprivation.

- Install a **10,000 lux SAD lamp** in the staff break area. A 30-minute exposure during break time can meaningfully improve mood and energy. Cost: €40–80. Impact: significant.
- Maximise natural light in work areas. Clean windows (grime reduces light transmission by 20–30%). Keep blinds open. Position the espresso station near windows if possible.
- Use **daylight-spectrum bulbs** (5000K+) in staff areas during winter. The warm 2700K lighting that creates atmosphere for customers is wrong for staff who need light therapy.
- Check that back-of-house areas (prep kitchen, storage, office) are adequately lit. Dark staff spaces compound the outdoor darkness.

**Temperature:**
- Keep the staff area genuinely warm — not just tolerable. A warm break room with a comfortable chair is a small act of care that costs almost nothing.
- Provide hot drinks and food during shifts. A bowl of soup at the staff lunch break during January is morale in a bowl.
- Allow warm layers. If your uniform policy requires a specific apron but not a specific under-layer, let people wear warm clothing underneath.

**Scheduling:**
- Where possible, avoid assigning the same person to every early-dark opening shift. Rotate so nobody is always arriving in full darkness.
- Be flexible with shift swaps during the worst months (December–February). If someone is struggling, a later start can make a real difference.
- Protect breaks absolutely. In winter, a skipped break is not just tired staff — it is staff losing their only chance to sit under a SAD lamp.

**Commute support:**
- If budget allows, consider a small transport allowance during the coldest months. Even €30/month towards bus passes or fuel acknowledges the reality of a Baltic commute.

sections.heading: Morale Through the Dark Months
sections.body:
Winter morale requires deliberate effort. In summer, it takes care of itself — sunshine, terrace, tourists, energy. In January, you have to manufacture it.

**Use T220-02 (Winter Morale Activity Calendar):**

**January:**
- Team dinner or outing in the first week (celebrate surviving Christmas rush)
- Set a team goal for the month (not revenue — something fun: "Every team member learns to pour a tulip" or "We try 5 new recipes this month")
- Bring in treats: a cake, doughnuts, a surprise on a particularly dark Monday morning

**February:**
- Valentine's Day special menu — involve the team in creating it
- Mid-winter team event: bowling, cinema, board game night, sauna visit
- Start planning the spring menu together — gives people something to look forward to

**March:**
- Celebrate the equinox (March 20): daylight is returning. Mark it visibly — change the music, bring flowers into the cafe, acknowledge the shift
- Begin terrace preparations — even cleaning outdoor furniture signals that winter is ending
- Schedule any training that was postponed: barista skills, cupping, food safety refresher

**Daily morale habits:**
- The shift highlight: end every shift by naming one good thing someone did
- Music matters more in winter — curate playlists that are warm and uplifting without being aggressive
- Keep the cafe feeling alive: fresh flowers, seasonal decorations, candles (even battery-powered ones). A cafe that looks alive feels alive.
- Coffee tastings for the team: open a new single-origin, taste together, discuss. It takes 10 minutes and reconnects people with why they work in coffee.

The owner's energy sets the tone. If you are visibly drained and dreading January, the team will mirror it. If you acknowledge that winter is hard but show up with warmth and intention, they will too.

sections.heading: Using Winter for Growth
sections.body:
Winter is quiet. Revenue drops. The temptation is to see this as a problem to endure. The reframe: it is an investment period. The months when you cannot grow revenue are the months you grow capability.

**Training programmes:**
The barista who is too busy to attend training in July has time in January. Schedule:
- Grinder calibration and shot diagnosis workshops
- Latte art practice sessions (weekly 30-minute sessions)
- Food safety certification renewals
- New brewing method training (pour-over, AeroPress, siphon)
- Customer service role-play and complaint handling practice

**Menu development:**
Use quiet days to test new menu items. Have the team develop and propose dishes or drinks. This gives them creative ownership and produces better results than the owner deciding alone.

Winter menus should lean into comfort: spiced drinks, hot chocolate with house-made marshmallows, hearty soups, warming pastries. Develop these as a team project in November for December launch.

**Deep cleaning and maintenance:**
Schedule the deep clean that never happens during busy months:
- Behind and under all equipment
- Grinder burr replacement
- Espresso machine professional service
- Fridge and freezer defrost and deep clean
- Storage room reorganisation
- Repaint scuffed walls

**Strategy and planning:**
The quiet of January is ideal for the annual business review (see "Decisions, Not Guesses" article). Revenue data from the previous year is complete. You have mental space to think strategically. Use it.

**Physical improvements:**
Minor renovations, furniture repairs, signage updates — all easier when the cafe is half-empty. The customers who return in spring find a refreshed space.

sections.heading: The Spring Reset
sections.body:
March in Lithuania is a turning point. Daylight increases rapidly — from 10 hours in early March to 13 hours by month end. Temperatures climb from -5°C to +5°C. Snow melts. The psychological shift is enormous.

**Marking the transition:**

Do not let spring arrive unnoticed. Mark it:
- **Change the menu.** Swap the winter specials for spring items. Lighter flavours, fresher ingredients, the first iced drinks.
- **Change the music.** Brighter, more energetic playlists signal the shift before customers consciously notice.
- **Clean the exterior.** Winter leaves salt stains, grime, and clutter. A clean exterior in March says "we are waking up."
- **Plan terrace opening.** Even if outdoor seating does not work until May, cleaning and positioning furniture in April creates anticipation — for staff and customers.

**Spring hiring:**
If you need summer terrace staff, start recruiting in March. Good baristas get hired early. Waiting until May means choosing from whoever is left.

Spring is also when you lose people. Staff who endured winter may leave in April — they stayed for the quiet months and now want a change. The annual leave calendar fills up. Be proactive: have the one-to-one conversations in March. Ask: "Are you happy? What do you need? Are you staying for summer?" Better to know in March than be surprised in May.

**The energy lift:**
The team that made it through a Baltic winter together has a bond. Acknowledge it: "We survived another one. The terrace opens in six weeks. Let's make this summer our best yet."

The cafe that treats winter as an investment period — training, developing, planning, caring for people — enters summer stronger than the one that just endured it. Winter is where the quiet work happens. Spring is where it pays off.
